cataloging_subject.label: Le Morte Darthur (Caxton recension)
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The late-fifteenth-century Middle English Arthurian prose compilation attributed to Sir Thomas Malory as represented by William Caxton's 1485 recension, including Caxton's preface, chapter table, and twenty-one-book/507-chapter organizing framework inherited by Sommer volume 1 and the MaloryWks2 encoding. This cataloging unit is not Sommer's complete three-volume edition, a facsimile of either surviving Caxton copy, or the materially different Winchester version. Caxton's editorial book and chapter divisions are transmission structure, not silently treated as Malory's original authorial divisions.

catalog_summary.title: Le Morte Darthur
catalog_summary.author: Sir Thomas Malory (self-named; probably Thomas Malory of Newbold Revel, but exact historical identification remains debated)
catalog_summary.editor: William Caxton (1485 recension and twenty-one-book framework); H. Oskar Sommer (1889 source edition)
catalog_summary.date: Completed between 4 March 1469 and 3 March 1470; first printed 31 July 1485

assertion.notes: Caxton's 1485 colophon applies this title to the whole printed book.

titles.value: The Hoole Book of Kyng Arthur and of His Noble Knyghtes of the Rounde Table
notes: The ending calls the work the whole book of King Arthur and his noble knights; the scholarly project distinguishes this Malory-associated wording from Caxton's conventional whole-book title.
Common modernized title spelling; the preferred title retains Caxton's form Darthur.

agents.name: Sir Thomas Malory
notes: Active in the fifteenth century. The text names its author as Sir Thomas Malory and the Winchester colophons call him a knight prisoner. Identification with Thomas Malory of Newbold Revel is well supported but retained as a historical identification question rather than encoded as certain identity.

agents.name: William Caxton
notes: Caxton lived approximately 1422-1491/1492.

agents.name: H. Oskar Sommer
notes: Born 1861; the sources consulted for this record did not establish a death date.

scope.description: Authorship of the Arthurian prose compilation, distinct from certainty about which historical Thomas Malory bore that name

notes: The responsibility is confirmed for the self-named Sir Thomas Malory; the biographical identification remains separately qualified.

scope.description: Selection and combination of multiple French and English Arthurian narratives
Reduction, translation, reshaping, and original combination of source narratives into Middle English prose
The 1485 print recension, including preface, chapter table, and division into twenty-one books and 507 announced chapters

notes: Caxton's own colophon says that he divided the work into twenty-one books, chaptered it, printed it, and finished it at Westminster.

scope.description: Editorial responsibility for the 1889 source-edition representation, not the medieval compilation's authorship
Page-for-page and line-for-line transcription of the Caxton text used by the U-M encoding

date_statements.display: Completed in the ninth regnal year of Edward IV, between 4 March 1469 and 3 March 1470
notes: The regnal-year statement dates completion of the text, not Caxton's later book/chapter divisions.

date_statements.display: 31 July 1485, printed by William Caxton at Westminster

scope.description: The Caxton recension represented by MaloryWks2

places.label: Westminster
notes: Recorded as the historical printing place in Caxton's colophon.

assertion.notes: The British Library catalog names Newgate Prison, while the De Montfort scholarly account says probably the Tower of London; London is more secure than the exact prison.

scope.description: First printed publication, not place of composition

language_statements.label: Middle English
notes: The pinned XML header's language declaration lists only Latin, but the complete intellectual text is overwhelmingly Middle English; direct content inspection and the British Library catalog control.

language_statements.label: Latin

scope.description: Fifteen explicitly tagged Latin phrases, headings, and quotations in the pinned representation

notes: Embedded Latin does not change the work's overall prose form or primary language; the epitaph called a ‘vers’ makes Book XXI locally mixed.

form_statements.description: A large Middle English prose compilation. Book XXI is locally mixed because chapter 7 embeds the Latin epitaph that Caxton calls a ‘vers’, but this brief insertion does not make the whole cataloging unit mixed-form.

genre_statements.label: Arthurian compilation
notes: Compilation describes source-combination and cataloging structure without deciding the disputed modern question of absolute artistic unity.

subject_statements.label: Death, repentance, and the fall of the Arthurian fellowship

summaries.value: Malory's Middle English prose compilation gathers and reshapes French and English Arthurian narratives into the rise, flourishing, spiritual testing, fracture, and fall of King Arthur's fellowship. Its interlaced stories include Arthur's birth and establishment of the realm, the careers of Lancelot, Gareth, Tristram, Galahad, Percival, Bors, and many other knights, the quest for the Holy Grail, the love of Lancelot and Guinevere, Mordred's rebellion, Arthur's last battle and departure, and the penitential deaths of the survivors.

The encoded Caxton recension contains Caxton's preface and chapter table followed by twenty-one books: early Arthur and Merlin (Books I-IV), the Roman campaign (V), Lancelot and Gareth (VI-VII), the long Tristram complex (VIII-X), Galahad's conception and Lancelot's madness (XI-XII), the Grail quest (XIII-XVII), Lancelot and Guinevere (XVIII-XIX), and the destruction and aftermath of Arthur's realm (XX-XXI).

This record follows the Caxton recension's preface/table/twenty-one-book boundary because that is the exact intellectual presentation encoded by MaloryWks2. It does not project Caxton's divisions backward as Malory's undisputed original structure, and it treats the Winchester manuscript as a related, independent version rather than an upstream source.

extent.description: The intellectual extent is 507 announced chapters under twenty-one book sections. Book XXI chapter 7 embeds one Latin epitaph that Caxton calls a ‘vers’, so that chapter is counted as mixed; semantic verse-line count is unknown because neither the XML nor Caxton's print encodes lineation. The pinned XML has 23 top-level DIV1 units (preface, table, and 21 books) but only 502 actual DIV2 chapter containers: Book I has 25 containers for 28 announced chapters, Book IV has 28 for 29, and Book VII has 35 for 36 because of combined, missing, or unlisted chapter structures. The other eighteen books' container counts equal their announced counts. The 502 encoding containers are therefore preserved as representation data rather than substituted for the 507-chapter intellectual extent.

content_parts.label: Caxton's Preface
content_parts.summary: Caxton defends Arthur's historical reputation, describes receiving a copy of Malory's work, recommends its examples of virtue and avoidance of vice, and announces the twenty-one-book, 507-chapter plan.

content_parts.label: Caxton's Table or Rubric of Chapters
content_parts.summary: A book-by-book table summarizes the announced chapters of the twenty-one-book recension and exposes several discrepancies with the printed chapter sequence that the electronic editors document rather than erase.
notes: The table announces the same 507 chapters counted under the twenty-one book parts, so its rows are not counted a second time in the intellectual extent.

content_parts.label: Book I: Arthur's Birth and Early Reign
content_parts.summary: Uther Pendragon fathers Arthur through Merlin's device; Arthur is raised in concealment, proves his kingship through the sword in the stone, is crowned, fights the rebel kings, receives Excalibur, and sets in motion the tragedy of Mordred through the May Day children.
notes: Caxton announces 28 chapters; the pinned XML has 25 DIV2 containers because chapter organization and headings do not map one-to-one.

content_parts.label: Book II: The Tale of Balin and Balan
content_parts.summary: Balin draws the fatal sword, helps overthrow King Rience, strikes the Dolorous Stroke at King Pellam's castle, and unknowingly kills and is killed by his brother Balan.

content_parts.label: Book III: Arthur's Marriage and the Round Table
content_parts.summary: Arthur marries Guinevere, receives the Round Table, and sends Gawain, Tor, and Pellinore on quests whose judgments establish both knightly fellowship and its moral hazards.

content_parts.label: Book IV: Merlin, Morgan le Fay, and Knightly Adventures
content_parts.summary: Merlin is imprisoned through his desire for Nimue; Arthur defeats five kings and survives Morgan le Fay's plot through Accolon; Gawain, Ywain, Marhaus, and Pelleas pursue interwoven adventures.
notes: Caxton announces 29 chapters; the pinned XML has 28 DIV2 containers.

content_parts.label: Book V: Arthur's Roman War
content_parts.summary: Arthur rejects Roman tribute, kills the giant of Mont Saint-Michel, defeats Emperor Lucius, campaigns through Europe, and is crowned emperor.

content_parts.label: Book VI: Sir Lancelot du Lake
content_parts.summary: Lancelot and Lionel seek adventures; Lancelot escapes four enchantress queens, defeats Sir Turquyn, frees prisoners, enters the Chapel Perilous, and returns to court with his reputation enlarged.

content_parts.label: Book VII: The Tale of Sir Gareth of Orkney
content_parts.summary: Gareth arrives anonymously as Beaumains, endures Lynet's contempt, defeats the colored knights and the Red Knight of the Red Lands, reveals his identity, wins Lyoness, and is reconciled with his kin.
notes: Caxton announces 36 chapters; the pinned XML has 35 DIV2 containers.

content_parts.label: Book VIII: Sir Tristram de Lyones
content_parts.summary: Tristram's birth, education, combat with Marhaus, healing by Isode, love potion, service to King Mark, early rivalry with Palomides, and widening fellowship with Arthurian knights begin the long Tristram sequence.

content_parts.label: Book IX: La Cote Male Taile and Tristram
content_parts.summary: The adventures of La Cote Male Taile and Maledisant lead back into Tristram's conflicts with Palomides and King Mark, his exile and madness, and his encounters with Arthur and Lancelot.

content_parts.label: Book X: Tristram and Other Adventures
content_parts.summary: The largest Caxton book interlaces Tristram and Isode, Palomides, Lamorak, Dinadan, Lancelot, King Mark, tournaments, factional violence, and Joyous Gard, ending with the deferred combat between Tristram and Palomides.

content_parts.label: Book XI: Lancelot and Galahad
content_parts.summary: At Corbenic Lancelot is deceived into begetting Galahad on Elaine; Bors witnesses the child and the Grail, Guinevere's jealousy drives Lancelot into madness, and Percival and Ector encounter Grail miracles.

content_parts.label: Book XII: Lancelot's Madness and Recovery
content_parts.summary: The mad Lancelot wanders, fights, and suffers before Elaine recognizes him and the Grail heals him; he lives at Joyous Isle, is restored to his companions, and the Tristram sequence closes with Palomides' baptism.

content_parts.label: Book XIII: The Quest of the Holy Grail Begins
content_parts.summary: Galahad is knighted, occupies the Siege Perilous, draws the sword from the stone, receives the white shield, and joins the Round Table's vow to seek the Grail; Lancelot begins penitential recognition of his failures.

content_parts.label: Book XIV: Percival's Grail Trials
content_parts.summary: Percival receives instruction on the Grail knights, survives supernatural dangers and allegorical visions, resists demonic temptation, and undertakes bodily penance.

content_parts.label: Book XV: Lancelot's Grail Trials
content_parts.summary: Lancelot encounters holy signs and visions, learns from hermits that Galahad is his son, and confronts the spiritual consequences of his worldly attachments.

content_parts.label: Book XVI: Gawain, Ector, Bors, and Lionel
content_parts.summary: Gawain and Ector's visions expose their failures, while Bors accepts penance, rescues a maiden instead of his brother, resists temptation, and survives Lionel's murderous rage.

content_parts.label: Book XVII: The Achievement of the Holy Grail
content_parts.summary: Galahad, Percival, Bors, and Percival's sister travel by the miraculous ship; her sacrificial death enables a healing; the Grail knights reach Corbenic, heal the Maimed King, and achieve the Grail before Galahad and Percival die.

content_parts.label: Book XVIII: Lancelot, Guinevere, and Elaine of Astolat
content_parts.summary: After the Grail quest Lancelot returns to Guinevere; he clears her of a poisoning charge, fights at the Winchester tournament wearing Elaine of Astolat's sleeve, and receives the dead maiden's letter after her unreturned love destroys her.

content_parts.label: Book XIX: Guinevere's Abduction and the Healing of Urry
content_parts.summary: Meliagaunt abducts Guinevere; Lancelot rides in the cart to rescue her, secretly visits her chamber, defeats her accuser, and later miraculously heals Sir Urry after the other Round Table knights fail.

content_parts.label: Book XX: The Most Piteous Tale of the Morte Arthur
content_parts.summary: Agravain and Mordred expose Lancelot and Guinevere; Lancelot's rescue of the queen kills fellow knights and splits the Round Table, Arthur wars against Lancelot, and the dying Gawain seeks reconciliation as Mordred usurps the realm.

content_parts.label: Book XXI: Arthur's Last Departure and the End of the Fellowship
content_parts.summary: Mordred's rule, the final battle, Arthur's departure with the queens, Guinevere's convent life, and Lancelot's penitence and death close the fellowship; chapter 7 embeds Arthur's Latin epitaph, and Malory's prayer and Caxton's dated colophon end the book.
notes: Chapter 7 introduces ‘Hic iacet Arthurus Rex quondam Rex que futurus’ as a ‘vers’. It is embedded inline in prose, and no semantic verse-line divisions are encoded.

related_works.title: The Hoole Book of Kyng Arthur and of His Noble Knyghtes of the Rounde Table (Winchester version)
notes: The Winchester and Caxton versions descend from a shared earlier textual layer but differ materially; neither surviving version is modeled as a direct copy of the other.

related_works.title: Lancelot-Grail (Vulgate Cycle)
notes: One major French source complex; the relation varies by tale and passage.

related_works.title: Prose Tristan
notes: The principal source complex for the long Tristram sequence, with other materials interlaced.

related_works.title: Alliterative Morte Arthure
notes: A major Middle English source for the Roman War material and an influence on Malory's style.

related_works.title: Stanzaic Morte Arthur
notes: A Middle English source for the final Lancelot-Guinevere and Arthurian-collapse narratives, alongside French material.

evidence.locator: HEADER; Caxton's preface and table; DIV1/DIV2/HEAD/P/FOREIGN elements; Malory ending and Caxton colophon
evidence.summary: Supplies the exact cataloging boundary, titles, author and editor statements, completion and print dates, twenty-one-book/507-chapter announcement, book summaries, Middle English prose text, embedded Latin, and immediate source-edition statement.

evidence.citation: Element, chapter-container, page-range, language-tag, and top-level-part audit of the pinned MaloryWks2 XML, 15 July 2026.
evidence.locator: All DIV1, DIV2, HEAD, PB, EPB, P, L, and FOREIGN elements and NODE attributes
evidence.summary: Counts 23 top-level DIV1 units, 502 DIV2 chapter containers, 15 Latin FOREIGN elements, 1307 P, and no L. It maps every preface/table/book unit to printed pages and shows that Books I, IV, and VII account for the 507-intended versus 502-container discrepancy.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Library Digital Collections, Le Morte Darthur, item MaloryWks2.
evidence.summary: Confirms the current electronic-item title, public contents and text, source-edition context, and Arthurian subject access.

evidence.citation: MaloryWks2 lineage evidence for Sommer volume 1 and its two scan parts.
evidence.summary: Supports Sommer's editorial/transcription responsibility, the 1889 source-edition relationship, page-faithful structure, and printed page ranges for all content parts.

evidence.citation: MaloryWks2 lineage evidence for Caxton's 1485 print state and the two surviving copies.
evidence.summary: Supports the exact print date and place, Caxton colophon title and editorial role, surviving-copy model, Manchester base-copy provenance, and distinction between print content and modern facsimiles.

evidence.citation: MaloryWks2 lineage evidence for British Library Additional MS 59678 and its relationship to the Caxton recension.
evidence.summary: Supports the Winchester witness date, Middle English language, southern-English origin, London composition claim, incomplete extent, and independent parallel-version model.

evidence.citation: De Montfort University Centre for Textual Studies, ‘Thomas Malory's Le Morte Darthur: The Life and Work.’
evidence.summary: Supports the knight-prisoner self-description, probable identification with the Newbold Revel Malory, 1469-1470 completion, probable London prison context, the two divergent versions and shared original, and distinction between Malory's whole-book wording and Caxton's title.

evidence.citation: Kara L. McShane, ‘Malory's Morte d'Arthur: Exhibition Guide,’ Rossell Hope Robbins Library Digital Projects, University of Rochester, 2010.
evidence.summary: Provides a scholarly overview of the fifteenth-century Arthurian prose romance, its narrative scope, major French and Middle English source complexes, Caxton's editorial role, surviving copies, and 1976 Morgan facsimile.

evidence.citation: Debra Yee, ‘Eugène Vinaver's Magnificent Malory: Exhibit Guide,’ Robbins Library Digital Projects, University of Rochester.
evidence.locator: Sections on Caxton/Winchester differences and the debate over eight separate tales versus one unified work
evidence.summary: Documents Winchester's distinct structure, Caxton's editorial intervention, Vinaver's multi-tale thesis, and later arguments for qualified unity; it supports cataloging this item as a compilation while keeping the unity question explicit.

evidence.citation: Ralph Norris, Malory's Library: The Sources of the Morte Darthur (Boydell & Brewer, 2008), publisher description and contents via Cambridge Core.
evidence.summary: The current book description identifies the work as an eclectic adaptation drawing on major and minor sources and treats the eight tale divisions recognized by the standard edition; it cautions against a simplistic one-source lineage.

open_questions.question: Which historical Sir Thomas Malory wrote the compilation?
next_steps: Retain authorship under the self-named Sir Thomas Malory while qualifying the Newbold Revel identification.
Review new biographical and archival scholarship before normalizing exact life dates or a unique authority identity.

open_questions.question: In which London prison, and over what exact span, was the compilation written?
next_steps: Preserve London as probable and distinguish completion date from total composition span.
Resolve the British Library's Newgate claim against the De Montfort account's probable Tower of London context through current biographical scholarship.

open_questions.question: Should Malory's text be described primarily as one unified book, eight tales, or a compilation whose unity and plurality coexist?
next_steps: Keep unit_type compilation and preserve the exact Caxton recension boundary without treating Caxton's 21 books as Malory's authorial structure.
Review current work-structure scholarship before modeling nested intellectual tales beneath the Caxton books.

open_questions.question: What exact source text, manuscript, and passage underlies every portion of Malory's compilation?
next_steps: Use current tale- and passage-level source studies before expanding adaptation relations.
Do not flatten the Vulgate, Post-Vulgate, Prose Tristan, and Middle English sources into a single direct source.

open_questions.question: Can every difference between Caxton's 507 announced chapters and the pinned XML's 502 DIV2 containers be reconciled to exact combined, missing, duplicated, or unlisted chapter structures?
next_steps: Audit table rubrics, printed chapter headings, and XML DIV2 boundaries for Books I, IV, and VII against the complete Rylands facsimile.
Keep 507 as intellectual extent and 502 as encoding representation until the boundary audit is fully documented.

notes: Content parts use normalized catalog labels while retaining Caxton's exact book sequence, intended chapter totals, XML NODE identifiers, Sommer printed pages, and Manchester IIIF canvas ranges.

The 23 content parts are paratext plus twenty-one Caxton books. Extent records 507 intellectual chapters under those 21 book sections and does not count the preface or chapter-table rows as additional chapters; the pinned encoding represents 502 DIV2 chapter containers.
Middle English is primary despite the pinned header's incomplete Latin-only declaration; fifteen tagged Latin passages are preserved as embedded-language metadata.
Manuscript dates, physical descriptions, facsimile access, and image rights remain in the linked lineage manifest unless needed here to qualify intellectual-work metadata.
